segments.inferno-13-g12: The subject is still the Harpies from the preceding context; Dante puts the object “Ali” before the verb “hanno” for descriptive emphasis. “Late” is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning “broad,” agreeing in feminine plural with “Ali.”
segments.inferno-13-g13: This continues the list of their physical features, with the verb “hanno” understood from the previous segment. The repeated “e ... e ...” is polysyndetic, piling up details: “and necks and human faces.”
segments.inferno-13-g14: Again the verb “hanno” is understood: they have “piè con artigli,” literally “feet with talons.” “Piè” is a shortened poetic form for “piedi,” here plural in sense though singular-looking in form.
segments.inferno-13-g15: The description continues elliptically: they have “’l gran ventre” feathered. “’l” is the elided form of “il,” and “pennuto” agrees with “ventre,” though it precedes the noun for poetic compression.
segments.inferno-13-g16: Here the sentence shifts from physical description to action: “fanno lamenti” means “they make laments” or “utter wailings.” The phrase “in su li” is an old expanded form of modern “sugli,” meaning “upon/on the.”
segments.inferno-13-g17: “E ’l buon maestro” introduces Virgil as the speaker, with “’l” as elided “il.” Inside the quotation, “Prima che” takes the subjunctive “entre,” an archaic form of “entri,” meaning “before you enter/go in farther”; the listed senses for “entre” do not match this verbal use.
segments.inferno-13-g18: “Sappi” is a second-person singular imperative of “sapere”: “know.” “Se’” is the contracted form of “sei,” and “nel secondo girone” locates Dante in the second ring of the seventh circle.
segments.inferno-13-g19: This reporting clause interrupts the direct speech: “mi cominciò a dire” means “he began to say to me.” “Mi” is an indirect object, and “a dire” is the infinitive construction governed by “cominciò.”
segments.inferno-13-g20: The direct speech resumes with “e sarai,” where the complement is understood: “and you will be/remain in it.” “Mentre che” here has the force of “as long as/until,” leading to the future “verrai”; “ne l’” is the separated older spelling of modern “nell’.”
segments.inferno-13-g21: “Però” has its older causal/result meaning “therefore,” not the modern adversative “however.” “Riguarda” is an imperative meaning “look,” and “ben” functions adverbially: “well,” that is, carefully.
segments.inferno-13-g22: “Sì” means “so/then,” linking the command to its result: if Dante looks carefully, he will see. “Torrien” is an archaic conditional plural, “would take away,” and “fede al mio sermone” means trust or credibility from my discourse.
segments.inferno-13-g23: “Sentia” and “vedea” are archaic imperfect forms for modern “sentivo” and “vedevo.” “D’ogne parte trarre guai” depends on “sentia”: literally, “I heard wails drawn forth from every side”; after the negative “non vedea persona,” the relative clause takes the subjunctive “facesse,” “who did/made it.”
segments.inferno-13-g24: “Per ch’io” is a contraction of “per che io,” meaning “wherefore/for which reason I.” “Tutto smarrito” is an adverbial-adjectival phrase meaning “utterly bewildered,” and “m’arrestai” is reflexive in form: “I stopped myself / came to a halt.”
segments.inferno-13-g1: The clause uses the pluperfect construction “era ... arrivato” with “essere” as auxiliary: “had arrived.” “Ancor” means “yet,” and “di là” is an adverbial phrase meaning “over there/on the other side,” placed before the subject “Nesso” in poetic word order.
segments.inferno-13-g2: “Quando” introduces the time clause that follows the preceding pluperfect: while Nessus had not yet arrived, “we set out.” “Ci mettemmo” is a reflexive idiom meaning “we set ourselves/started off,” and “per un bosco” means “through a wood”; the relative clause “che ... era segnato” describes the wood, with “da neun sentiero” meaning “by no path.”
segments.inferno-13-g3: This is an elliptical descriptive phrase: an implied verb such as “there was” must be supplied from the following pattern. “Non” negates the expected feature of the wood, and the singular “fronda” can function collectively as “foliage.”
segments.inferno-13-g4: “Ma” contrasts with the preceding negative and has the force of “but rather.” “Di color fosco” means “of dark color”; “color” is an apocopated form of “colore,” not the pronoun “coloro.”
segments.inferno-13-g5: This repeats the elliptical pattern: “not straight branches,” again with an implied “there were.” “Schietti” here describes branches that would be plain, clean, or upright in form, hence “straight.”
segments.inferno-13-g6: “Ma” supplies the contrasting positive description after the negated noun phrase. “Nodosi e ’nvolti” are plural adjectives agreeing with the implied “rami”; “’nvolti” is an elided poetic form for “involti,” here meaning twisted or wound around.
segments.inferno-13-g7: “V’eran” is the contraction of “vi eran,” meaning “there were.” The phrase again negates the expected natural feature of trees: there were no “pomi,” that is, fruit.
segments.inferno-13-g8: The final “ma” gives the grim substitute for fruit: “stecchi con tòsco.” “Con” indicates that the twigs bear or contain poison, and “tòsco” is an archaic word for poison.
segments.inferno-13-g9: The main verb is “han,” an apocopated form of “hanno,” and the subject “quelle fiere selvagge” is delayed until after the object phrase “sì aspri sterpi né sì folti.” “Sì ... né sì ...” means “so ... nor so ...,” while the relative clause “che ’n odio hanno” means “which hold in hatred,” with “’n” standing for “in”; “i luoghi cólti” means the cultivated lands between Cecina and Corneto.
segments.inferno-13-g10: “Quivi” locates the action: “there.” The subject is “le brutte Arpie,” and “lor” is an archaic/poetic possessive meaning “their”; “fanno” takes “nidi” as its object, literally “make their nests.”
segments.inferno-13-g11: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to the Harpies. “Cacciar” is an apocopated poetic form of “cacciarono,” simple past “drove out,” with “i Troiani” as object; “de le Strofade” means “from the Strophades,” and “con tristo annunzio di futuro danno” explains the means or circumstance: with a sad prophecy of future harm.
segments.inferno-13-g25: “Cred’ ïo” is “credo io,” with the final vowel dropped and the subject placed after the verb for poetic emphasis: “I think.” The nested clauses use “ch’”/“che” as “that”; “credette” is passato remoto, while “credesse” and the apocopated “uscisser” for “uscissero” are imperfect subjunctives governed by the reported belief.
segments.inferno-13-g26: “Tra quei bronchi” is a prepositional phrase modifying where the voices seemed to come out. “Quei” means “those,” and “bronchi” here is an archaic/poetic word for branches or boughs, not the modern anatomical sense.
segments.inferno-13-g27: “Da gente” gives the imagined source of the voices: “from people.” The relative clause “che per noi si nascondesse” uses singular “nascondesse” with collective “gente”; “si” is reflexive, and “per noi” means on account of us or from fear of us.
segments.inferno-13-g28: “Però” has its older causal value, “therefore,” linking the master’s action to the mistaken idea just described. “Disse” is passato remoto, and “’l” is the contracted article “il” before “maestro.”
segments.inferno-13-g29: “Se” introduces a real conditional clause with present “tronchi,” “if you break off.” “Qualche fraschetta” is singular after “qualche” but means “some little twig,” and “d’una d’este piante” means “from/of one of these plants,” with “este” an archaic form of “these.”
segments.inferno-13-g30: “Li pensier” is archaic for “i pensieri,” “the thoughts,” with “pensier” apocopated. “C’hai” is “che hai,” “that you have”; “si faran” is “si faranno,” a future middle/reflexive form meaning “will become,” and “monchi” figuratively means truncated or incomplete.
segments.inferno-13-g31: “Allor” means “then,” and “porsi” is an archaic poetic first-person past form meaning “I stretched/put forth.” “Colsi” is passato remoto of “cogliere,” here “I plucked,” and “ramicel” is an apocopated diminutive, “little branch/twig”; “da un gran pruno” gives the source, “from a large thornbush.”
segments.inferno-13-g32: “E ’l” is “and il,” with the article contracted. “Tronco suo” uses poetic word order with the possessive after the noun, meaning “its trunk,” and “gridò” is passato remoto, “cried out.”
segments.inferno-13-g33: “Perché” introduces a direct question meaning “why.” “Mi” is the direct object, “me,” and “schiante” is an archaic/poetic second-person present form of “schiantare,” “you break/splinter.”
segments.inferno-13-g34: This segment contains only sentence-final punctuation and no grammatical words to parse.
segments.inferno-13-g154: The interrogative che means “what” and modifies colpa: literally, “what fault/blame have I?” Ho is present indicative, with the explicit subject io added for emphasis. De la is the archaic/poetic form of della, “of/for,” and tua vita rea means “your wicked/sinful life.”
segments.inferno-13-g155: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the end of the quoted question.
segments.inferno-13-g156: Quando introduces a temporal clause, “when.” ’l is the contracted form of il, so ’l maestro means “the master,” referring to Virgil. Fu ... fermo is passato remoto of essere plus an adjective, meaning “was/stood still” or “had stopped”; sovr’ esso is poetic contraction for sopra esso, “above/over him or it.”
segments.inferno-13-g157: Disse is passato remoto, third-person singular, of dire, “he said.” It introduces Virgil’s direct question.
segments.inferno-13-g158: Chi is interrogative, “who.” Fosti is actually the second-person singular passato remoto of essere, “you were,” though the supplied lemma list treats it incorrectly; together Chi fosti means “Who were you?”
segments.inferno-13-g159: Che is a relative pronoun referring back to chi, “who/that.” Per tante punte means “through so many points/openings/wounds”; per marks passage through something. Soffi is the present indicative “you breathe/blow out,” and doloroso sermo is an archaic/Latinate phrase for “sorrowful speech,” accompanied con sangue, “with blood.”
segments.inferno-13-g160: This segment contains only punctuation, closing the sentence after the quoted question.
segments.inferno-13-g35: Da che functions as a temporal conjunction, “after/when.” The participle fatto is placed before fu in poetic word order; together they mean “was made/became,” with di sangue bruno giving the material/cause of the dark color, “with dark blood.”
segments.inferno-13-g36: Ricominciò is passato remoto, “he/it began again” or “resumed,” with the subject understood as the wounded tree/branch. A dir is the normal construction ricominciare a + infinitive, and dir is the apocopated poetic form of dire.
segments.inferno-13-g37: Perché introduces a direct question, “why?” The clitic mi is the direct object before the second-person singular verb scerpi, “you tear/rend,” addressed to Dante.
segments.inferno-13-g38: Non ... alcuno forms a negative-polarity construction: literally “do you not have any,” naturally “have you no.” Tu is expressed after hai for emphasis, while spirto is a poetic shortened form of spirito and di pietade specifies what kind of spirit is meant, “of pity.”
segments.inferno-13-g39: Uomini is a predicate noun placed first for emphasis: “men/human beings we were.” Fummo is the first-person plural passato remoto of essere, presenting their former human state as a completed fact.
segments.inferno-13-g40: E or contrasts the past with the present; or is the poetic shortened form of ora, “now.” Siam is siamo, and siam fatti sterpi means “we have been made / have become brushwood,” with fatti acting as a participle or predicative adjective.
segments.inferno-13-g41: Ben intensifies the statement, roughly “surely” or “indeed.” Dovrebb’ is elided dovrebbe, followed by the infinitive esser; la tua man is the subject, and più pia is the feminine adjective phrase agreeing with man, meaning “more compassionate.”
segments.inferno-13-g42: Se introduces a hypothetical condition, but here its listed senses do not include the conjunction “if.” State fossimo is inverted poetic order for fossimo state, the pluperfect subjunctive of essere, “we had been”; anime di serpi is the predicate complement, “souls of serpents.”
segments.inferno-13-g43: Come begins the first half of a simile, answered later by sì, “so.” D’un stizzo verde means “from/of a green firebrand,” and ch’arso sia uses a relative clause with subjunctive passive/perfect force; da l’un de’ capi means “at/from one of the ends,” with de’ contracted from de + i.
segments.inferno-13-g44: Che continues the relative description of the firebrand. Da l’altro means “from the other end,” with capo understood; geme here has the special sense of exuding/oozing moisture, while cigola describes the hissing or crackling caused per vento che va via, “by/through air that escapes.”
segments.inferno-13-g45: Sì is the correlative “so,” completing the comparison begun by Come. De la scheggia rotta is a fronted source phrase, “from the broken splinter,” and usciva is imperfect; the compound subject parole e sangue follows the verb, with insieme stressing that words and blood came out at the same time.
segments.inferno-13-g46: Ond’ is the contracted form of onde, here meaning “whereupon/therefore,” linking Dante’s reaction to what he has just seen and heard. Lasciai is passato remoto, and lasciare + infinitive forms a permissive/causative construction: la cima is placed between lasciai and cadere, “I let the tip fall.”
segments.inferno-13-g47: E coordinates this final reaction with lasciai. Stetti is from stare, “I stood/remained,” though the supplied lemma-senses do not match that verb; come introduces a comparison, and l’uom che teme is a generic singular, “a man/person who is afraid.”
segments.inferno-13-g134: This target contains only a punctuation mark and no grammatical material to analyze.
segments.inferno-13-g135: “E” coordinates this figure with the one just mentioned, while “l’altro” means “the other one,” with the article elided before a vowel. The phrase is the subject of the main verb “gridava” later in the sentence.
segments.inferno-13-g136: “Cui” is a relative pronoun used with an indirect sense, literally “to whom,” because “parere” takes the person affected as an indirect object: “to whom it seemed.” “Pareva” is imperfect, and “tardar troppo” is an infinitive phrase meaning that his own lingering or slowness seemed excessive to him.
segments.inferno-13-g137: “Gridava” is the imperfect indicative, describing an ongoing or vivid past action: “was shouting” or “cried out.” It is the main verb whose subject is “l’altro.”
segments.inferno-13-g138: “Lano” is a direct vocative, naming the person addressed in the shouted speech. The comma marks the pause after the address.
segments.inferno-13-g139: “Sì” here means “so,” modifying the whole negative comparison: his legs were not “so” cautious or alert at Toppo. “Furo” is an archaic poetic form of “furono,” “they were,” with “le gambe tue” as subject; “a le” is the older separated form of modern “alle,” and “dal Toppo” means “at/of Toppo,” referring to the battle or encounter mockingly called “giostre,” “jousts.”
segments.inferno-13-g140: This target contains only a punctuation mark and no grammatical material to analyze.
segments.inferno-13-g141: “Poi che” functions as a conjunction meaning “since” or “when,” not simply as separate “then” plus “that.” “Li” is an archaic dative pronoun equivalent to “gli,” “to him,” and “fallia” is an imperfect form meaning “was failing”; the subject of that verb is “la lena,” his breath or strength.
segments.inferno-13-g142: The prepositional phrase “di sé e d’un cespuglio” is fronted for emphasis and means “out of himself and a bush” or “of himself and a bush.” “Fece” is the simple past of “fare,” and “un groppo” is the object, a “knot” or tangled mass formed by his body and the bush.
segments.inferno-13-g68: This is the subject noun phrase, expanded by a relative clause: “La meretrice che...” = “the harlot who....” The double negative “mai ... non” has the force of “never,” and “torse” is a poetic preterite from torcere, “turned”; “li” is an archaic masculine plural article, while “putti” here means “shameless/foul,” not “children.”
segments.inferno-13-g69: This segment is an appositive description of “La meretrice,” identifying her as a universal ruin and a vice especially found in courts. “de le” is the older separated form of modern “delle,” “of the”; “corti” is a noun meaning “courts,” though the supplied lemma points to a different word.
segments.inferno-13-g70: Here the main verb finally appears: “infiammò” is passato remoto, “inflamed/enkindled.” The subject is still “La meretrice,” and “contra me” gives the direction of the hostility; “li animi tutti” means “all the spirits/minds,” with “tutti” reinforcing the totality.
segments.inferno-13-g71: “li ’nfiammati” is a substantivized past participle: “the inflamed ones,” that is, the people whose minds had been stirred up. “infiammar” is an apocopated poetic form for a finite past-tense verb, “they inflamed,” and “sì” introduces the degree that will be completed by the following “che.”
segments.inferno-13-g72: This is the result clause governed by “sì ... che”: “so ... that....” “tornaro” is a shortened poetic form of “tornarono,” and with “in” it means “turned into/became”; “’ lieti onor” has an elided article before the plural noun “onor.”
segments.inferno-13-g73: “L’animo mio” is the grammatical subject of the sentence, placed before several intervening phrases before its verb appears in “fece.” “Animo” here means the inner mind or soul, not physical courage.
segments.inferno-13-g74: This prepositional phrase gives the motive or cause: “per” means “through/because of.” “Disdegnoso gusto” is a compressed expression for a disdainful inclination or bitter satisfaction.
segments.inferno-13-g75: “Credendo” is a gerund, explaining the mistaken reasoning of the subject: “thinking that....” “col” contracts “con il,” here meaning “by the,” and both “morir” and “fuggir” are apocopated infinitives: “by dying to flee/avoid disdain.”
segments.inferno-13-g76: This is the main predicate: “fece” = “made,” with “me” as its object and “ingiusto” as a predicate adjective placed first for emphasis. “contra me giusto” means “against my just self,” creating the paradox that suicide made the speaker unjust toward himself though he was otherwise just.
segments.inferno-13-g77: “Per le nove radici” is an oath formula, “by the new roots”; “esto” is an archaic form of “this,” referring to the tree into which the speaker has been transformed. “Vi giuro che” introduces the sworn statement, and “già mai non” is an emphatic negative meaning “never”; “ruppi fede al mio segnor” means “broke faith with my lord.”
segments.inferno-13-g78: This relative clause modifies “mio segnor.” “Fu” is passato remoto of “essere,” and “d’onor ... degno” uses “di” after “degno”: literally, “worthy of honor”; “sì” intensifies the adjective, “so worthy.”
segments.inferno-13-g79: This is a conditional protasis: “if any one of you returns to the world.” “Di voi alcun” is partitive, “one of you,” and “riede” is an archaic present-tense form meaning “returns.”
segments.inferno-13-g80: “Conforti” is present subjunctive used jussively after the condition: “let him comfort/strengthen.” “La memoria mia” means not only personal memory but the speaker’s reputation, which he asks to have restored among the living.
segments.inferno-13-g81: This relative clause modifies “la memoria mia.” “Giace ancor del colpo” means that his memory still lies wounded or prostrate because of the blow, and in “che ’nvidia le diede,” the relative “che” is the object of “diede,” while “le” refers back to the feminine noun “memoria.”
segments.inferno-13-g48: This is a counterfactual if-clause: archaic “S’” stands for “se” (“if”), and “avesse potuto” is the pluperfect subjunctive, followed by the apocopated infinitive “creder” for “credere.” “Elli” is an archaic form meaning “he,” and “prima” means “beforehand,” linking this clause to the later conditional “non averebbe ... distesa.”
segments.inferno-13-g49: This is the reporting clause inserted inside the speech. “Rispuose” is an archaic form of “rispose,” and “’l savio mio” means “my sage/wise guide,” with the article “il” elided after the preceding vowel.
segments.inferno-13-g50: “Anima lesa” is a direct address to the speaking tree-soul. The feminine adjective “lesa” agrees with “anima” and means wounded or injured, both physically in the branch and spiritually in its condition.
segments.inferno-13-g51: “Ciò c’ha veduto” means “that which he has seen”: here “c’” is a contraction of “che,” not the pronoun “ci.” “Ha veduto” is the perfect tense, and “pur con la mia rima” means “only through my verse,” with “con” expressing the means by which Dante had known such a thing before.
segments.inferno-13-g52: This is the main clause of the counterfactual condition: “non averebbe ... distesa” is archaic for “non avrebbe ... distesa,” a conditional perfect, “would not have stretched out.” “La man” is an apocopated form of “la mano,” and “in te” has the sense of “against/upon you.”
segments.inferno-13-g53: “Mi fece indurlo” uses “fare” causatively: literally, “made me induce him,” with “lo” referring to Dante. “Ad ovra” means “to a deed/work,” and the relative clause “ch’a me stesso pesa” says that this deed weighs upon, or burdens, Virgil himself.
segments.inferno-13-g54: “Dilli” is not the modern verb suggested by the lemma here, but an imperative “di’” plus old dative “li,” meaning “tell him.” “Chi tu fosti” is an indirect question, with “fosti” as the past definite of “essere”: “who you were.”
segments.inferno-13-g55: “Sì che” introduces a purpose/result clause, and “rinfreschi” is subjunctive: “so that your fame may be renewed.” “’N vece d’alcun’ ammenda” means “in place of some amends,” while “nel mondo sù” means the living world above Hell.
segments.inferno-13-g56: “Dove” refers back to “nel mondo sù.” “Tornar li lece” has an impersonal construction: “it is permitted to him to return,” with “li” functioning as an old dative pronoun meaning “to him.”
segments.inferno-13-g57: This is an elliptical reporting phrase: the verb of speaking is understood from the following direct speech. “’L tronco” identifies the speaker as the tree-trunk, the visible form of the soul.
segments.inferno-13-g58: “Sì ... ch’” forms a correlative construction: “so ... that.” “Col” contracts “con il,” “dir” is a substantivized infinitive meaning “speech/speaking,” and “m’adeschi” means “you lure/entice me,” with “m’” standing for “mi.”
segments.inferno-13-g59: The “ch’” introduces the result of the preceding “sì”: “that I cannot be silent.” “I’” is an abbreviated poetic form of “io,” and “posso tacere” uses “potere” plus infinitive, “am able to be silent.”
segments.inferno-13-g60: “Voi non gravi” is an optative or polite subjunctive-like expression: “may it not be burdensome to you.” “Perch’ ïo ... m’inveschi” gives the reason, and “a ragionar” means “in speaking/talking,” with the reflexive “m’inveschi” picturing the speaker as getting caught or entangled in his own discourse.
segments.inferno-13-g61: “Io son colui che” means “I am he who,” introducing the speaker’s self-identification. “Tenni” is the past definite of “tenere,” “I held,” and “ambo le chiavi del cor di Federigo” is a metaphor for intimate control over Frederick II’s confidence and decisions.
segments.inferno-13-g62: The second relative clause repeats “che,” still referring to “colui”: “and who turned them.” “Le” is a feminine plural object pronoun referring back to “le chiavi,” and “volsi” is the past definite of “volgere,” “I turned.”
segments.inferno-13-g63: The two gerunds explain how he turned the keys: by locking and unlocking. They continue the key metaphor, describing his power to close or open access to the emperor.
segments.inferno-13-g64: “Sì” is correlative with the following “che,” meaning “so.” “Soavi,” formally plural to match “chiavi,” functions adverbially in sense: he turned the keys so gently or skillfully.
segments.inferno-13-g65: This “che” introduces the result clause: he turned the keys so well that he excluded almost everyone from Frederick’s private counsel. The word order is poetic: “quasi ogn’uom” is the object of “tolsi,” while “dal secreto suo” states what they were removed from.
segments.inferno-13-g66: “Fede portai” has object-before-verb order: literally, “faithfulness I bore,” meaning “I was faithful.” “Offizio” is an archaic spelling of “ufficio,” here meaning the high office or service entrusted to him.
segments.inferno-13-g67: “Tanto ch’” introduces the consequence of that devotion: “so much that.” “Perde’” is truncated for “perdei,” “I lost,” while “ne” means “from it/because of it”; “li sonni e ’ polsi” means sleep and pulses, a poetic way of saying rest and health, even life-force.
segments.inferno-13-g103: Quando introduces a temporal condition, here with a general force: when or whenever this happens. Si parte is the reflexive/pronominal form of partire, meaning “departs” or “separates itself,” and ond’ is an archaic contraction for onde, “from which”; s’è disvelta is a reflexive perfect with essere as auxiliary, “has torn itself away.”
segments.inferno-13-g104: Minòs is the subject and la manda is “sends it/her,” with la referring back to l’anima, though the supplied lemma lists it as an article. A la is the older uncontracted form of modern alla, and settima foce means the seventh opening or mouth of Hell, namely the seventh circle.
segments.inferno-13-g105: Cade has the soul from the previous sentence as its understood subject. In la is an archaic separated form where modern Italian would usually contract to nella, but here the sense is motion “into the forest.”
segments.inferno-13-g106: The phrase non l’è parte scelta means “no place is chosen for it”: l’ is a dative pronoun, not the article suggested by the lemma list. È scelta forms a passive construction with essere plus past participle, while parte here means a place or allotted spot.
segments.inferno-13-g107: Ma contrasts the lack of choice with the action of fortune or chance. Là dove means “there where” or “wherever,” and la is again a pronoun referring to the soul; balestra is used as a verb, “shoots/hurls,” despite the noun senses listed.
segments.inferno-13-g108: Quivi resumes là dove: “there, in that very place.” Germoglia is present tense, continuing the general rule, and come introduces a simile; gran is poetic for grano, “grain,” not the adjective “great” listed in the senses.
segments.inferno-13-g109: Surge, an archaic form of sorge, means “rises” or “grows up,” with the same understood subject, the soul-seed. In vermena e in pianta silvestra expresses transformation into a young shoot and then into a wild plant.
segments.inferno-13-g110: L’Arpie is the subject of the following finite verb fanno. The elided article l’ stands for le before the vowel of Arpie, “the Harpies.”
segments.inferno-13-g111: Pascendo is a gerund, “feeding,” giving the means or circumstance by which the Harpies cause the following effect. De le is the older separated form of delle, and sue refers to the plant’s leaves, hence the soul’s own leaves.
segments.inferno-13-g112: Fanno takes dolore as its object: literally “they make pain,” idiomatically “they cause pain.” The present tense keeps the description in the timeless, habitual mode of this punishment.
segments.inferno-13-g113: The verb fanno is understood again: the Harpies make “a window/opening for the pain.” Al dolor is dative, “for the pain,” meaning that the wound provides an outlet through which the pain can be expressed.
segments.inferno-13-g114: Come l’altre means “like the others,” referring to the other souls who will recover their bodies at the Last Judgment. Verrem is an apocopated future form of verremo, “we shall come,” and per nostre spoglie means “for our mortal remains,” with the article omitted poetically before nostre.
segments.inferno-13-g115: Ma non però ch’ means “but not however that,” introducing a denied consequence. Alcuna is “any one” of the souls, sen is se ne, “with/of them,” and rivesta is present subjunctive after che: “may clothe herself again.”
segments.inferno-13-g116: Ché gives the reason: “for/because.” Aver is an infinitive used as the subject of è giusto, and ch’om si toglie means “that which one takes from oneself,” with om as an impersonal “one” and si as reflexive.
segments.inferno-13-g117: “Qui” is placed first for emphasis: this is the place to which the bodies will be dragged. “Le” is a feminine plural object pronoun here, referring to the bodies/spoils mentioned just before; “strascineremo” is future tense, first-person plural, “we shall drag.”
segments.inferno-13-g118: “Per la mesta selva” means “through the mournful wood,” with “per” indicating movement throughout the place. “Saranno ... appesi” is a future passive construction with “essere” plus past participle: “our bodies will be hung.”
segments.inferno-13-g119: “Ciascuno” refers back to “i nostri corpi”: each body will be hung separately. “Al prun de l’ombra sua molesta” uses archaic/poetic “de” for “di” and means “on the thornbush belonging to its grievous shade”; “sua” agrees with “ombra” but refers to each body’s own shade or soul.
segments.inferno-13-g120: “Noi eravamo ... attesi” is an imperfect construction meaning “we were attentive/intent,” with “attesi” used adjectivally. “Al tronco” gives the object of their attention, “to the trunk,” using “a” after the idea of attending or paying heed.
segments.inferno-13-g121: “Credendo” is a gerund, “thinking/believing,” explaining why they remained attentive. “Ch’altro ne volesse dire” has imperfect subjunctive “volesse” after the mental verb “credendo”; “altro” means “something else/more,” and “ne” means “about it.”
segments.inferno-13-g122: “Quando” introduces the sudden event interrupting their waiting. “Fummo ... sorpresi” is a passive-like past construction, “we were surprised/startled,” and “d’un romor” gives the cause or agent: “by a noise”; “romor” is a poetic shortened form of “rumore.”
segments.inferno-13-g123: “Similemente a colui” sets up a comparison: they were startled like a person in a hunting scene. “Che venire sente ’l porco e la caccia” is a relative clause; with a verb of perception, the infinitive “venire” depends on “sente,” so the sense is “who hears the boar and the chase coming.”
segments.inferno-13-g124: “Ch’ode” continues the relative description of “colui,” the person at his post. “Ode” is an archaic/literary present form meaning “he hears,” and “le bestie” are the animals of the hunt.
segments.inferno-13-g125: This phrase continues the objects of “ode”: he hears not only the beasts but also “le frasche stormire.” The infinitive “stormire” functions after the perception verb, with “le frasche” as what is heard rustling.
segments.inferno-13-g126: “Ed ecco” is a vivid presentational formula, “and behold/and there were,” with the verb of appearance left implicit. “Da la sinistra costa” means “from the left side/slope”; “da la” is the older separated form of modern “dalla.”
segments.inferno-13-g127: “Nudi e graffiati” are plural masculine adjectives modifying the implied “due,” the two figures just introduced. The pair of adjectives describes their condition as they appear: naked and scratched.
segments.inferno-13-g128: “Fuggendo” is a gerund showing the action accompanying their arrival: “while fleeing.” “Sì forte” means “so strongly/violently” and prepares the following result clause introduced by “che.”
segments.inferno-13-g129: “Che” introduces the result of their violent flight: they were running so hard that they broke through obstacles. “Rompieno” is an archaic imperfect third-person plural form, equivalent to “rompevano,” and “ogne” is an old form of “ogni,” “every.”
segments.inferno-13-g130: “Quel dinanzi” is elliptical: literally “that one in front,” meaning the foremost of the two fugitives. The verb of speaking is omitted before the direct quotation, a common compressed narrative style.
segments.inferno-13-g131: “Or” means “now,” adding urgency to the cry. “Accorri” is a second-person singular imperative addressed directly to Death: “hasten here” or “come quickly.”
segments.inferno-13-g132: The repeated imperative “accorri” intensifies the desperation of the speaker. The command remains second-person singular, still addressed to the personified Death.
segments.inferno-13-g133: “Morte” is a direct address to personified Death, the addressee of the imperatives. The supplied lemma points to “morto,” but here the form is the noun “death,” not the adjective “dead.”
segments.inferno-13-g143: “Di rietro a loro” is a poetic way of saying “behind them”; the locative phrase is placed first for emphasis. The subject “la selva” comes after the verb “era,” and “piena di” governs the complement “nere cagne,” meaning “full of black hounds.”
segments.inferno-13-g144: “Bramose” and “correnti” are plural feminine adjectives/participles agreeing with “cagne.” “Ch’uscisser” is elided from “che uscissero,” an imperfect subjunctive used in the simile after “come,” suggesting hounds as if just released from a chain.
segments.inferno-13-g145: “In quel che” means “into that one who,” referring to the person who had crouched or hidden. “S’appiattò” is reflexive past historic, while “miser li denti” means “they set their teeth”; “miser” is an apocopated form of “misero,” and “li” is an archaic plural article.
segments.inferno-13-g146: “Quel” here functions as the object, “that one” or “him.” “Dilaceraro” is a shortened poetic form of “dilacerarono,” past historic third-person plural, and “a brano a brano” is an adverbial phrase meaning “piece by piece.”
segments.inferno-13-g147: “Poi” continues the sequence of actions. “Sen portar” stands for “se ne portarono,” with clitics meaning they carried the limbs away; “portar” is a shortened past-historic form, and “quelle membra dolenti” is its direct object.
segments.inferno-13-g148: “Presemi” is “prese mi,” meaning “took me,” with the object pronoun attached to the verb in poetic style. The subject follows the verb, “la mia scorta,” and “per mano” is the idiom “by the hand.”
segments.inferno-13-g149: “Menommi” is “menò mi,” meaning “led me,” again with the object pronoun attached. “Al” contracts “a il,” “che” introduces a relative clause referring to “cespuglio,” and “piangea” is the imperfect “was weeping”; “per le rotture sanguinenti” means through or by means of its bleeding breaks, with “in vano” as an adverbial phrase.
segments.inferno-13-g150: “O” marks direct address, like English “O” or “oh.” “Iacopo” is the person being addressed in the vocative.
segments.inferno-13-g151: “Dicea” is a poetic imperfect form of “diceva,” meaning “was saying.” It is inserted parenthetically between parts of the direct quotation.
segments.inferno-13-g152: “Da Santo Andrea” completes the name as an origin or by-name: Jacopo from Sant’Andrea. “Santo Andrea” is a place-name built from “Saint Andrew.”
segments.inferno-13-g153: “Che” is interrogative here, meaning “what.” “T’è giovato” is “ti è giovato,” an impersonal perfect construction with a dative pronoun: “what has it profited you?” The infinitive phrase “di me fare schermo” means “to make/use me as a shield,” with “di me” placed before the infinitive for emphasis.
segments.inferno-13-g82: “Un poco” is an adverbial phrase meaning “a little” or “for a little while.” “Attese” is a preterite verb form from attendere, here with the implied subject “the poet”: “he waited.”
segments.inferno-13-g83: “E poi” links the next action, “and then.” “Da ch’el si tace” means “since/now that he is silent”: “el” is an archaic form for “he,” and “si tace” is a pronominal form of tacere, literally “keeps himself silent.”
segments.inferno-13-g84: The verb “disse” is the preterite of dire, “said.” The subject follows the verb in poetic order: “’l poeta” = “the poet,” and “a me” gives the indirect object, “to me.”
segments.inferno-13-g85: “Non perder” is a negative imperative-like infinitive: “do not lose/waste.” “L’ora” literally means “the hour,” but here means the available moment or time.
segments.inferno-13-g86: “Ma” contrasts with the preceding prohibition and has the force of “rather.” “Parla” is a second-person singular imperative: “speak.”
segments.inferno-13-g87: “E” coordinates this command with “parla.” “Chiedi a lui” is another second-person singular imperative construction: “ask him,” with “a lui” marking the person addressed.
segments.inferno-13-g88: “Se” is the conditional conjunction “if,” though the supplied sense list does not include that value. “Più ti piace” uses piacere impersonally: literally “if it pleases you more,” meaning “if you prefer.”
segments.inferno-13-g89: “Ond’” here means “therefore/whereupon,” connecting Dante’s response to Virgil’s suggestion. The verb “said” is omitted but understood: “I to him,” i.e. “I said to him.”
segments.inferno-13-g90: “Domandal” is a contraction of “domanda-lo,” a second-person singular imperative meaning “ask him.” “Di quel che credi ch’a me satisfaccia” means “about what you think will satisfy me”; “satisfaccia” is a present subjunctive after “credi che,” expressing the content of Virgil’s judgment.
segments.inferno-13-g91: “Ch’” introduces the reason, “because/for.” “I’” is the reduced poetic form of “io,” not the article suggested by the lemma list, and “potrei” is conditional: “I would be able/could.”
segments.inferno-13-g92: “Tanta pietà” is the subject phrase, “so much pity/compassion.” “M’accora” means “distresses me” or “weighs on my heart”; the elided “m’” is the object pronoun “me.”
segments.inferno-13-g93: “Perciò” means “therefore,” referring to Dante’s inability to ask because of pity. “Ricominciò” is preterite and means “he resumed/began again,” with Virgil as the implied subject.
segments.inferno-13-g94: “Se” is used in an optative formula, not an ordinary conditional: “so may the man...” “L’om” means “the man,” referring to Dante, and “ti faccia liberamente ciò che ’l tuo dir priega” means “may he freely do/grant for you what your speech requests”; “faccia” is subjunctive.
segments.inferno-13-g95: “Spirito incarcerato” is a direct vocative address to the soul in the tree. “Incarcerato” is a past participle used adjectivally: “imprisoned” or “confined.”
segments.inferno-13-g96: “Ancor ti piaccia” continues the courteous subjunctive request: “may it still/further please you.” “Di dirne come...” introduces the infinitive complement, “to tell us how”; “l’anima si lega in questi nocchi” uses “si lega” as a reflexive or middle-passive form, “the soul becomes/is bound in these knots,” and “nocchi” means tree-knots or gnarls, a sense absent from the supplied list.
segments.inferno-13-g97: “E” coordinates a second request with the previous one. “Dinne” is a contraction of “di’ + ne,” an imperative meaning “tell us/of it.”
segments.inferno-13-g98: “Se tu puoi” is a parenthetical condition: “if you can.” “Puoi” is the present indicative, second-person singular, of potere.
segments.inferno-13-g99: “S’” here is the conjunction “se,” “whether/if,” before “alcuna,” not the reflexive pronoun indicated by the lemma list. “Alcuna mai” means “any one ever,” and “di tai membra si spiega” means “unfolds/disengages itself from such limbs,” with “si spiega” used reflexively.
segments.inferno-13-g100: “Allor” means “then/thereupon.” “Soffiò” is preterite, “blew” or “breathed out,” and “il tronco” is the subject; “forte” functions adverbially, “strongly/hard.”
segments.inferno-13-g101: “E poi” advances the sequence: “and then.” “Si convertì ... in” is a pronominal construction meaning “turned into/was transformed into,” with “quel vento” as subject and “cotal voce” as the resulting form, “such a voice.”
segments.inferno-13-g102: “Brievemente” means “briefly.” “Sarà risposto a voi” is an impersonal passive-like future construction: literally “it will be answered to you,” meaning “you will be answered briefly.”
segments.inferno-13-g161: The verb of speaking is omitted after “a noi,” a common poetic ellipsis: literally, “And he to us,” meaning “And he said to us.” “Elli” is an archaic/poetic subject pronoun for “he,” referring to the speaker in the bush.
segments.inferno-13-g162: “Anime” is addressed directly with the vocative “O,” and “che” introduces a relative clause: “souls who...” The compound verb is inverted poetically as “giunte siete” for modern “siete giunte,” with the feminine plural participle agreeing with “anime”; “a veder” gives the purpose or result of their arrival. “C’ha” is not “ci ha” here but a contraction of “che ha,” and “sì da me disgiunte” means “so separated from me,” with “sì” intensifying the participle.
segments.inferno-13-g163: “Raccoglietele” is an imperative addressed to the two travelers, formed from “raccogliete” plus the enclitic object pronoun “le,” referring back to the torn-off leaves. “Al piè del...” means “at/to the foot of,” with “piè” as a poetic shortened form of “piede”; “cesto” here means the bush or shrub-body of the speaker.
segments.inferno-13-g164: “I’” is the apocopated poetic form of “Io,” the subject of “fui.” “De la città” means “from/of the city,” identifying the speaker as Florentine. The relative clause “che... mutò” refers to the city: it “changed” or “exchanged” its first patron, Mars, for “il Batista,” Saint John the Baptist.
segments.inferno-13-g165: “Ond’” means “therefore” or “from which fact,” drawing a consequence from Florence’s change of patron. “Ei” refers to the displaced first patron, Mars; “la” is the object pronoun referring to the city. “Farà trista” is a future-tense construction with a predicate adjective: “will make her sorrowful/wretched.”
segments.inferno-13-g166: This begins a contrary-to-fact conditional protasis: “se non fosse che...” means “if it were not that...,” with “fosse” in the imperfect subjunctive. “’N sul” is a contraction for “in sul,” meaning “on/at the,” and “alcuna vista” means some remaining visible trace or image of “lui,” Mars.
segments.inferno-13-g167: “Que’ cittadin” is a shortened poetic form for “quei cittadini,” and it supplies the subject of the later verb “avrebber.” The first “che” introduces a relative clause, “who later refounded her,” where “la” refers to the city; the second “che” modifies “cener,” “the ashes that remained.” “Sovra ’l” means “upon the,” with elision of the article.
segments.inferno-13-g168: This is the apodosis of the conditional begun with “se non fosse”: “avrebber” is the shortened poetic form of “avrebbero.” “Avrebber fatto lavorare” is a causative/periphrastic expression, literally “would have had [work] done” or “would have caused labor,” meaning their rebuilding would have been in vain.
segments.inferno-13-g169: “Fei” is an archaic first-person preterite form of “fare,” meaning “I made.” “A me” is reflexive in sense, “for myself,” and “de le mie case” means “from/out of my houses”; the line confesses that the speaker hanged himself in his own home.

sentences.inferno-13-s5: They have broad wings, and human necks and faces, feet with talons, and their great belly feathered; they make laments upon the strange trees.
sentences.inferno-13-s6: And the good master began to say to me, “Before you go farther in, know that you are in the second ring, and you will remain so until you come to the horrible sandy expanse.”
sentences.inferno-13-s7: Therefore look carefully; then you will see things that would take credibility away from my speech.”
sentences.inferno-13-s8: I heard wails being drawn forth from every side, and I saw no person who was making them; wherefore, utterly bewildered, I stopped.
sentences.inferno-13-s1: Nessus had not yet arrived on the other side when we set out through a wood that was marked by no path.
sentences.inferno-13-s2: There was no green foliage, but foliage of a dark color; no straight branches, but knotty and twisted ones; no fruit was there, but dry twigs with poison.
sentences.inferno-13-s3: Those wild beasts that hate the cultivated places between Cecina and Corneto do not have brambles so rough or so dense.
sentences.inferno-13-s4: There the foul Harpies make their nests, who drove the Trojans from the Strophades with a mournful prophecy of future harm.
sentences.inferno-13-s9: I think he thought that I believed that so many voices were issuing, among those branches, from people who were hiding from us.
sentences.inferno-13-s10: Therefore the master said: “If you break off some little twig from one of these plants, the thoughts you have will all be cut short.”
sentences.inferno-13-s11: Then I stretched my hand a little forward and plucked a twig from a great thornbush; and its trunk cried out: “Why do you break me?”
sentences.inferno-13-s45: What blame do I bear for your wicked life?”
sentences.inferno-13-s47: When the master had stopped above him, he said: “Who were you, that through so many wounds you breathe out, with blood, sorrowful speech?”
sentences.inferno-13-s13: After it had then become dark with blood, it began again to say: “Why are you tearing me?”
sentences.inferno-13-s14: Have you no spirit of pity at all?
sentences.inferno-13-s15: We were men, and now we have been made brushwood: your hand surely ought to be more compassionate, even if we had been souls of serpents.
sentences.inferno-13-s16: As from a green firebrand that has been burned at one end, and from the other oozes and crackles because of the air escaping, so from the broken splinter came forth words and blood together; whereupon I let the tip fall, and stood like a man who is afraid.
sentences.inferno-13-s38: And the other, who thought he was lingering too long, cried: “Lano, your legs were not so wary at the jousts of Toppo!”
sentences.inferno-13-s40: And then, since perhaps his breath was failing him, he made a knot of himself and a bush.
sentences.inferno-13-s21: The harlot who never turned her shameless eyes away from Caesar’s household, the common death and vice of courts, inflamed all minds against me; and those inflamed ones so inflamed the Emperor that my happy honors turned into mournful griefs.
sentences.inferno-13-s22: My mind, out of disdainful pleasure, thinking to escape disdain by dying, made me unjust against myself, though I was just.
sentences.inferno-13-s23: By the new roots of this tree, I swear to you that I never broke faith with my lord, who was so worthy of honor.
sentences.inferno-13-s24: And if any one of you returns to the world, let him strengthen my memory, which still lies prostrate from the blow that envy dealt it.”
sentences.inferno-13-s17: “If he had been able to believe beforehand,” answered my wise guide, “wounded soul, what he has seen only through my verses, he would not have stretched out his hand against you; but the incredible nature of the thing made me induce him to do a deed that weighs heavily even on myself.”
sentences.inferno-13-s18: But tell him who you were, so that, in place of some amends, he may refresh your reputation in the world above, where he is permitted to return.”
sentences.inferno-13-s19: And the trunk said: “You so entice me with your sweet speech that I cannot be silent; and may it not trouble you if I get myself a little entangled in talking.
sentences.inferno-13-s20: I am the one who held both keys to Frederick’s heart, and who turned them, locking and unlocking, so gently that I kept almost every man away from his secrets; I bore faithfulness to the glorious office, so much so that because of it I lost sleep and the pulses of life.
sentences.inferno-13-s29: When the violent soul departs from the body from which it has torn itself away, Minos sends it to the seventh mouth.
sentences.inferno-13-s30: It falls into the forest, and no place is chosen for it; but wherever fortune hurls it, there it sprouts like a grain of spelt.
sentences.inferno-13-s31: It rises as a shoot and as a wild plant: the Harpies, then feeding on its leaves, cause pain and make an opening for the pain.
sentences.inferno-13-s32: Like the others, we shall come for our bodies, but not so that any one of us may put them on again, for it is not just to have what one takes from oneself.
sentences.inferno-13-s33: Here we shall drag them, and through the mournful wood our bodies will be hung, each on the thornbush of its own grievous shade.”
sentences.inferno-13-s34: We were still intent upon the trunk, thinking that it wished to tell us more of it, when we were startled by a noise, like one who at his post hears the boar and the hunt coming, and hears the beasts and the branches rustling.
sentences.inferno-13-s35: And behold, two came from the left side, naked and scratched, fleeing so violently that they broke through every barrier of the wood.
sentences.inferno-13-s36: The one in front said: “Now come, come, Death!”
sentences.inferno-13-s41: Behind them the forest was full of black she-hounds, ravenous and running like greyhounds that had broken loose from the chain.
sentences.inferno-13-s42: Into the one who had hidden himself they set their teeth, and they tore him apart piece by piece; then they carried off those suffering limbs.
sentences.inferno-13-s43: Then my guide took me by the hand and led me to the bush that was weeping in vain through its bleeding wounds.
sentences.inferno-13-s44: “O Jacopo,” it was saying, “of Sant’Andrea, what good has it done you to make a shield of me?”
sentences.inferno-13-s25: He waited a little, and then, “Since he is silent,” the poet said to me, “do not waste the time; rather, speak, and ask him, if that pleases you more.”
sentences.inferno-13-s26: Therefore I said to him: “Ask him again about what you think will satisfy me; for I could not, so much pity overcomes my heart.”
sentences.inferno-13-s27: Therefore he resumed: “So may the man freely grant you what your speech requests, imprisoned spirit; may it still please you to tell us how the soul is bound in these knots, and tell us, if you can, whether any ever unfolds itself from such limbs.”
sentences.inferno-13-s28: Then the trunk blew strongly, and then that wind turned into such a voice: “Briefly shall answer be made to you.”
sentences.inferno-13-s49: And he said to us: “O souls who have come to see the shameful mangling that has so separated my leaves from me, gather them at the base of the wretched bush.”
sentences.inferno-13-s50: I was from the city that exchanged its first patron for the Baptist; therefore he, because of this, will always make her sorrowful with his art; and if it were not that by the crossing over the Arno some sight of him still remains, those citizens who later refounded her upon the ashes left from Attila would have had their work done in vain.
sentences.inferno-13-s51: I made a gallows for myself out of my own houses.”
